Title: War in the Middle East: Iran ignores Trump ultimatum as tensions escalate
Article:
PARIS: The latest developments in the Middle East war:
- IRAN DISMISSES TRUMP ULTIMATUM -
Iran's central military command on Saturday brushed aside US President Donald Trump's threat to destroy the country's vital infrastructure if it did not accept a peace deal within 48 hours.
General Ali Abdollahi Aliabadi called Trump's ultimatum "a helpless, nervous, unbalanced and stupid action."
- TRUMP SAYS TEHERAN STRIKE KILLED MILITARY LEADERS -
Trump said on social media Saturday that "many" of Iran's military leaders were killed in a huge strike on the Islamic republic's capital.
"Many of Iran's Military Leaders, who have led them poorly and unwisely, are terminated, along with much else, with this massive strike in Teheran!" Trump said, accompanying the post with an undated video showing explosions lighting up a city's skyline.
- IRAQI SHIPPING ALLOWED TO TRANSIT HORMUZ, IRAN SAYS -
Iran will allow Iraqi shipping to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, its military command said, despite its broader blockade of the key sea lane at the mouth of the Gulf.
"We announce that our brotherly country of Iraq is exempt from any restrictions we have imposed on the Strait of Hormuz, and these restrictions only apply to enemy countries," spokesman Ebrahim Zolfaghari told state television.
- ISRAEL ARMY CLAIMS FIFTH MISSILE LAUNCHED FROM YEMEN -
The Israeli military said it had detected a missile launched from Yemen towards Israeli territory, the fifth such attack since the start of the Middle East war.
"Aerial defence systems are operating to intercept the threat", the military said.
- BLASTS OVER JERUSALEM -
Several loud explosions were heard over Jerusalem after the Israeli military warned it had detected missiles inbound from Iran.
AFP journalists heard at least six blasts. Earlier in the day, missile attacks on Tel Aviv and parts of central Israel wounded five Israelis, said the rescue services.
- US ARRESTS ALLEGED RELATIVES OF SLAIN IRANIAN GENERAL -
Two family members of slain Iranian general Qassem Soleimani have been arrested in the United States after their residency permits were rescinded, the US State Department said Saturday.
Federal agents detained the niece and grand niece of Soleimani, killed in a US drone strike in the Iraqi capital Baghdad in January 2020.
However, Iranian media spoke with two daughters of Soleimani who said the detained individuals were not related to the assassinated military leader.
- TRUMP THREATENS 'HELL' IF NO DEAL IN 48 HOURS -
Trump said Iran had 48 hours to make a deal on opening the vital Strait of Hormuz or face "hell."
"Remember when I gave Iran ten days to MAKE A DEAL or OPEN UP THE HORMUZ STRAIT," Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform.
"Time is running out – 48 hours before all Hell will reign (sic) down on them," the president said Saturday.
- ISRAEL HITS TYRE AFTER EVACUATION WARNINGS-
Israel's military renewed strikes on the southern Lebanese city of Tyre after issuing evacuation warnings, following attacks on nearby buildings that damaged a hospital.
Israel has carried out strikes across Lebanon and launched a ground invasion in the south after Hizbollah entered the war in the Middle East on the side of its backer Iran on March 2.
- TURKIYE'S ERDOGAN CALLS FOR END TO WAR -
Turkiye's President Recep Tayyip Erdogan said the war had led to a "geostrategic impasse" and called for new efforts to end the conflict, during a telephone conversation with Nato chief Mark Rutte, his office said.
- IRAN SAYS FIVE DEAD IN US-ISRAELI STRIKE -
US-Israeli strikes on Iran targeted a nuclear plant, a trade terminal, a cement factory and a petrochemical hub, with the latter killing five people, Iranian media reported.
A strike near the Bushehr nuclear power station killed a guard but caused no damage, the official IRNA news agency said. The UN's nuclear watchdog said no increased radiation levels had been detected.
"Five people were martyred following the American-Zionist enemy attack on some companies located in the Mahshahr Petrochemical Special Economic Zone," the ISNA news agency said, citing the deputy governor of Khuzestan province, Valiollah Hayati.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Don't pick sides in US-China rivalry': Sachs urges Malaysia
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia should not pick sides in the intensifying rivalry between Washington and Beijing, and keep an open, multi-partner approach to secure investment, technology and long-term growth.
Columbia University economics professor Jeffrey Sachs said forcing countries into exclusive alignments risks undermining their development prospects at a time when global economic power is shifting eastward.
"Malaysia's approach is: 'We're open to all of it. We're happy to partner with you, but we are open to China, India and others'. "There's no exclusivity here," he said in an interview on TV3's Money Matters, aired yesterday. "Being forced to choose, which unfortunately is an American mindset, that has to be resisted."
His remarks highlight the dilemma mid-sized economies face in navigating a fractured global order without getting caught in geopolitical crossfire.
For Malaysia, Sachs suggested the answer is in doubling down on strategic openness.
Central to this is leveraging regional cooperation, particularly through Asean and broader groupings such as the Regional Comprehensive Economic Partnership.
He said no country in the region can go at it alone, but collectively, Asean carries significant weight, both economically and demographically, giving it room to assert strategic autonomy.
"If Asean holds together strategically, that's 700 million people. That's twice the population of the United States. "So then you tell the US politely, 'thank you, but we're okay'. We have our own strategic autonomy. We don't take orders from anybody."
After centuries of Western dominance, he said the pendulum is swinging back to Asia, driven by rapid industrialisation, technological progress and demographic weight across the region.
"The biggest fundamental shift is the rise of Asia to become, once again, the centre of the world economy," he said. "For two millennia, Asia was the centre of the world economy. That era of Western dominance is now coming to an end."
Yet, even as Asia's economic rise gathers pace, the prevailing US mindset still views economic progress through a zero-sum lens, where one country's gain is another's loss, a perspective he said is fundamentally flawed.
"The nature of the world economy is that it's not a zero-sum struggle," he said. "But in the American mindset, it is... and that creates huge tensions."
Those tensions are increasingly spilling over into trade, technology and investment flows, with the US actively pushing countries to align with its technological ecosystem while excluding China's.
Sachs said such attempts to divide the global economy are unlikely to succeed, as countries ultimately gravitate towards cost, efficiency and access to innovation.
"I go around the world, talk to governments and they say, 'we'd like Chinese investment, but the Americans are threatening us'," he said. "I don't think this is going to work because what China offers is lower cost, high quality —very competitive."
Against this backdrop, Malaysia's strategy of remaining open to multiple partners could become a competitive advantage, as long as it is backed by strong domestic fundamentals.
Sachs said attracting high-value investment is not just about policy positioning, but also about building the right foundations at home, particularly in education, infrastructure and energy.
He cited East Asia's success, where strong education systems and technical expertise have driven decades of growth.
"Most important are the national skills. Is the workforce very well trained? Do you have high-quality engineers? "Make sure your educational offerings are superb and that you're at the cutting edge of science and technology."
At the same time, he said economic development is not accidental, but the result of a deliberate, long-term strategy, one that requires sustained investment across multiple fronts.
This includes not only physical infrastructure, such as transport and energy systems, but also human capital and digital capabilities, all of which must work in tandem to support growth.
"Development is a strategic activity. It's not something that just happens. The basics of the plan are investment in education, infrastructure and the business sector."
However, Sachs cautioned that the global financial system is currently not well-equipped to support such long-term development, particularly for poorer countries facing high borrowing costs and short-term debt structures.
He argued that a mismatch between short-term financing and long-term investment horizons remains a key constraint, calling for a rethink of global financial architecture to better support sustainable growth.
"The problem is not that the debt is so heavy, but that it's short term," he said. "Match your maturities with the length of time needed to produce economic results."
Beyond economics, Sachs flagged geopolitical conflict as the biggest threat to global stability —not only disrupting markets but also deterring investment and derailing development.
He pointed to ongoing tensions in the Middle East as an example of how quickly instability can ripple across regions, affecting everything from energy prices to investor confidence.
"The war is the No. 1 issue and I want these wars to stop. They make no sense," he said. "We have far more important things to do than to debate old conflicts... we have to cooperate."
He argued that economic resilience is as much about diplomacy as it is about policy, with regional cooperation and peaceful relations forming the bedrock of sustainable growth.
For countries like Malaysia, the path forward may not be simple, but Sachs emphasised that staying open, pragmatic and strategically independent is the best way to navigate an increasingly complex global landscape.
"Have a strategy and be nice to your neighbours. "Because regional cooperation, avoiding conflicts in your own neighbourhood, is really the best way to ensure safety."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'It started with massages': Children reveal care home horror
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Disturbing new testimonies have emerged from children who used to live in an unregistered  care home in Selayang here, which involve allegations of sexual abuse, systematic grooming and violence.
Four children have so far come forward to lift the veil on the horrors they experienced.
The sordid activities first came to light when a non-governmental organisation, Pertubuhan Kebangkitan Tiga Tangan Selangor (PKTTS), last week exposed the home's activities, which led to the arrests of four people, including a 31-year-old man who was the home's operator.
The children, who spoke to the
New Straits
Times
in the presence of the NGO's representatives, described being groomed  for illicit activities,  with the  perpetrators allegedly taking them to private condominiums.
There, the children were fed, allowed to swim and watch television before allegedly being subjected to abuse.
One 11-year-old boy claimed he was physically and sexually abused for two years.
"They would take us out to condominiums and treat us nicely at first. We were given food, allowed to swim and watch television.
"But later, it would start with things like giving massages before becoming something else. We were scared and didn't understand what was happening."
Another boy, 14, recalled how he was sexually abused by the home operator, both in the condominium and the man's home.
"He would take us there and let us play in the swimming pool. Once we went up to the apartment, he would drink alcohol.
"He would then apply chilli paste all over my body and ask me to dance like a girl. He would play some music videos on the television and ask me to follow the moves," the boy said.
The teen claimed that the care home operator's helper, a 25-year-old man, also abused him.
During the interview, the NST was shown several videos of the boys being forced to dress up like girls with make-up and dance to music.
The NST was also shown a video supposedly recorded by the care home operator showing two boys forced to engage in lewd acts.
Another victim, 17, said that he was not sexually abused but was severely beaten  after being caught smoking.
He claimed that he had to  miss two Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia (SPM) examinations due to head injuries after being beaten with a bamboo stick.
"I was bleeding profusely from the assault. I was in extreme pain and my vision became blurred, but all the care home operator did was give me some paracetamol and an energy drink.
"I was in bed for three days as I felt very dizzy and could barely open my eyes. This happened during the SPM period."
He said he asked to be brought to a clinic, but the care home helper just told him to apply coffee powder on his wounds to stop the bleeding.
"We were too afraid to tell anyone. If we tried to speak, we would be beaten. Even when we were injured, we were not taken to the hospital. That's why many stayed silent," he claimed.
PKTTS deputy president S. Sharwin said doctors at  Selayang Hospital, where the children were taken, confirmed that some injuries on the victims were sexual in nature and  were recently inflicted.
He said conversations with the children suggested that some had become inured to the abuse and  accepted them as part of life.
PKTTS president S. Maran said the videos  suggested a calculated, organised environment of abuse.
He said a Gombak Welfare Department officer had  visited the home several times and recorded statements from some of the children.
The department said it would seal the home tomorrow.
So far, 32 children had been returned to their families, while four would be relocated to other homes.
It was reported that at least nine children were subjected to sexual assault.
Five had been admitted to hospital for further examination.
The centre is believed to have  operated for nearly 10 years without registration.
The home operator, along with three others, have been remanded.
It is understood that the remand has been extended until Tuesday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: The charmer in command: Ling Liong Sik's three defining moments
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Three circumstances defined the cool, gentle and debonair character of Tun Dr Ling Liong Sik, who died yesterday  after a long and stormy political career. He was 82.
First, Dr Ling, in allying with Tan Sri Tan Koon Swan, fought a high-pressure war to wrest control of the MCA leadership in the mid-1980s from acting president Datuk Dr Neo Yee Pan, who assumed the presidency after Tan Sri Lee San Choon's sudden resignation as party chief and transport minister.
Lee's exit triggered a political imbroglio: Dr Neo, by then accused by rivals of inflating party membership, sacked Tan, Dr Ling and other allies but they were reinstated after a court battle.
In 1986, hardly a year after
he defeated Dr Neo by almost 80 per cent of votes, Tan fell from grace, convicted in Singapore over  the Pan-Electric Industries scandal.
As deputy president, Dr Ling immediately took over MCA and by becoming transport minister in January 1986, he set the stage for the second and extraordinary circumstance that materialised  in February 1988.
[[nid:1410493]]
He briefly helmed  Barisan Nasional as Tun Dr Mahathir Mohamad   dealt with Umno's registration crisis.
In his  "seat warmer" role, Dr Ling maintained  political stability, affirming support for Dr Mahathir, but failed in later years to control another MCA crisis —  forcing his withdrawal and leading to the third circumstance.
It was perhaps his bleakest moment: after he stepped down as transport minister in 2003, he was charged  in 2010 with  cheating the government over a land deal related to the PKFZ project. He  was acquitted by the High Court in 2013.
Despite the acquittal, his reputation suffered a dent. Still, MCA members and the political punditry of that period assessed Dr Ling as "politically shrewd" but "emotionally distant", rather than fiery or impulsive.
Of course, his 17-year tenure as transport minister was a mix of major infrastructure achievements and debacles, overseeing Port Klang's development and expansion into one of Southeast Asia's busiest ports while improving trade and logistics competitiveness.
Dr Ling positioned  Kuala Lumpur International Airport, opened in 1998, as a regional aviation hub and promoted privatisation of transport services and infrastructure projects, expanding highways, ports and aviation facilities.
Yet, he couldn't help being mired in details: he constantly "tweaked" transportation rules, like repeated changes  to the laws on vehicle window tinting.
But he had the good sense to install mandatory beepers on express buses, and stricter rules on dangerously overloading  lorries, now part of the system to mitigate highway crashes.
It reflected his philosophy as a believer in moderation and compromise, calm while his environment quaked.
[[nid:1410492]]
He was immersed in Chinese culture, mirroring his belief that every political move can be an elaborate form of Chinese opera.
If anything, Dr Ling displayed self-awareness and shared leadership: during the MCA leadership's squabbles, he preferred humility by philosophising that the "fish begins to rot at the head".
Dr Ling believed in pragmatism, navigating challenges calmly while managing complex inter-party relationships within BN.
No doubt, his supporters will characterise him as a "towering statesman" and a "nation builder", prioritising national infrastructure and human capital with a "steady hand" during Malaysia's heady economic transformation.
In a nutshell, Dr Ling's gentle and soft-spoken predisposition, while wielding firm leadership, made him approachable and affable, attested to by many senor journalists who were welcomed at his home for sudden interviews.
Dr Ling avoided "zero-sum" politics and rhetoric, preferring constructive negotiation and goodwill through  his distinct dead-pan expression and gravelly voice.
He was a charmer, not a
street fighter. One year, he crooned an American songbook classic from his office over RTM's TV1 to commemorate New Year's Eve.
Above all, his resilience stood out during his expulsion from MCA and intense internal factionalism in the proceeding years.
He was the last of towering political figures that included colleagues like Tun S. Samy Vellu and Tan Sri Rafidah Aziz — dominant and irreplaceable.
Dr Ling's  legacy put him as a key architect of Malaysia's modern transport infrastructure.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israel to strike key Lebanon-Syria border crossing, evacuation ordered
Article:
BEIRUT: The Israeli military said on Saturday it would strike an area near the main crossing between Syria and Lebanon, urging residents to evacuate immediately as it continued its attacks across Lebanon.
Israel has carried out strikes across Lebanon and launched a ground invasion in the south since March 2, when Hizbollah entered the war in the Middle East on the side of its backer Iran.
"Due to Hizbollah's use of the Masnaa Crossing for military purposes and smuggling of combat equipment, the (Israeli army) intends to carry out strikes on the crossing in the near future," said the military's Arabic-Language spokesman, Avichay Adraee.
"For your safety, we urge everyone near the crossing and anyone in the area marked in red on the map to evacuate immediately," he wrote on X, sharing the marked map.
A Lebanese security source at the Masnaa border crossing told AFP they were "currently evacuating the crossing following the Israeli threat."
In Syria, the General Authority for Borders and Customs' public relations director, Mazen Aloush, said the crossing, known as Jdeidet Yabous on the Syrian side, was "exclusively for civilian use and is not used for any military purposes."
Aloush added that "in light of the circulating warnings and out of concern for the safety of travellers, traffic through the crossing will be temporarily suspended until any potential risks subside."
The Masnaa crossing is the main crossing between Lebanon and Syria, making it a vital trade route for both countries and Lebanon's main land gateway to the rest of the region.
Israel previously struck the crossing in October 2024, during its previous war with Hizbollah.
The crossing remained closed until Lebanese and Syrian authorities began repair works after a ceasefire the following month.
- STRIKES ON TYRE -
In a statement, Lebanon's health ministry said a strike on Maaraka, near Tyre, killed five people and wounded one.
In previous statements, the ministry reported that an Israeli strike on Habbush killed at least two girls and wounded 22 people, while a strike on al-Hawsh near the coastal city of Tyre wounded 18, including a child, three women and three paramedics.
The strike on al-Hawsh damaged a nearby major hospital, according to the ministry.
The director of the Lebanese Italian Hospital told the state-run National News Agency (NNA) that it would "remain open to provide the necessary medical care" despite the damage.
Tens of thousands of people have left Tyre, but around 20,000 remain, including 15,000 displaced from surrounding villages, despite Israeli evacuation warnings covering most of the city and a swathe of the south.
Hours after the attack, the Israeli army struck three buildings in and around Tyre it had warned people to evacuate, according to the NNA.
Overnight strikes destroyed two buildings nearby, an AFP correspondent saw, shattering windows and also causing suspended ceilings to collapse in the hospital, management said.
The NNA also reported strikes across the south.
- 'UNACCEPTABLE' ATTACKS -
The Israeli military announced on Saturday the death of a soldier who "fell during combat in southern Lebanon", the 11th killed since the army began ground operations in the country.
It also announced having struck "more than 140 Hizbollah infrastructure targets" over the previous two days.
After attacking a bridge in the West Bekaa region in eastern Lebanon on Friday "to prevent the transfer of reinforcements and military equipment", Israel hit it again on Saturday, destroying it completely, the NNA said.
West Bekaa is right above Lebanon's south, where Israeli troops have been advancing on the ground.
Hizbollah claimed responsibility Saturday for a series of attacks on northern Israeli towns and Israeli troops in southern Lebanese towns near the border, particularly Marun al-Ras, Hula, Ainata and Bayada.
The war has displaced upwards of a million people in Lebanon and killed more than 1,400 people in the country, including 54 medics and three Indonesian UN peacekeepers in the south.
The United Nations force said on Friday that three peacekeepers were wounded in a blast inside a UN facility near Odaisse, and were rushed to hospital.
Jakarta slammed the incident as "unacceptable" after the UN office there confirmed the wounded were Indonesian.
On Saturday, a UN security official told AFP that Israeli forces destroyed 17 surveillance cameras linked to UNIFIL's main headquarters in Naqura.
The UN peacekeeping force has been caught in the crossfire in southern Lebanon since the start of the war, with Hizbollah launching attacks on Israel and its troops, and Israeli forces pushing into border towns.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US arrests relatives of slain Iran general Soleimani
Article:
WASHINGTON: Two family members of slain Iranian general Qassem Soleimani have been arrested in the United States after their residency permits were rescinded, the US State Department said Saturday.
"Last night, the niece and grand niece of deceased Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps Major General Qassem Soleimani were arrested by federal agents following Secretary of State Marco Rubio's termination of their lawful permanent resident (LPR) status," a department statement said.
It identified the niece as Hamideh Soleimani Afshar. Her daughter was not named.
Both "are now in the custody of US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE)," the statement said, without specifying their whereabouts.
But Iranian media quoted two daughters of Soleimani as saying the pair are not relatives.
"The US State Department's claim is a lie: the people arrested in the United States have no connection to the family," Zeinab Soleimani told the Fars news agency.
Another daughter, Narjes Soleimani, who is a member of Tehran's Islamic City Council, told Iranian state TV: "To this day, no member of the family nor any relative of Martyr Soleimani has resided in the United States."
The State Department did not immediately respond to a request for clarification from AFP.
Qassem Soleimani, who led the IRGC's foreign operations arm, was killed in a US drone strike while he was in the Iraqi capital Baghdad in January 2020 – the final year of President Donald Trump's first term in the White House.
"As identified by both press reporting and her own social media commentary, Soleimani Afshar is an outspoken supporter of the totalitarian, terrorist regime in Iran," the State Department said.
It also said she had "praised the new Iranian Supreme Leader" Ali Khamenei and "denounced America as the 'Great Satan.'"
Soleimani Afshar's husband has also been barred from entering the United States, the statement said.
The daughter and son-in-law of another slain Iranian official, security chief Ali Larijani, have also had their legal status terminated.
Both "are no longer in the United States and are barred from future entry," the statement said.
Larijani, secretary of Iran's Supreme National Security Council, was killed on March 17 in an Israeli strike.
"The Trump Administration will not allow our country to become a home for foreign nationals who support anti-American regimes," Rubio said in a post on X.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran rejects Trump's 'helpless, nervous' ultimatum
Article:
TEHRAN: Iran's central military command on Saturday rejected US President Donald Trump's
threat to destroy
the country's vital infrastructure if it does not accept a peace deal within 48 hours.
General Ali Abdollahi Aliabadi, in a statement from the Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters, said Trump's threat was a "a helpless, nervous, unbalanced and stupid action."
And, echoing the religious language of Trump's social media post, he warned that "the simple meaning of this message is that the gates of hell will open for you."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump gives Iran 48 hours to make deal or face 'hell'
Article:
WASHINGTON: President Donald Trump said on Saturday that Teheran had 48 hours left to cut a deal or face "hell", as US and Iranian forces scrambled to find a downed American airman.
Trump's latest threat came after a strike near an Iranian nuclear power plant prompted evacuations, and as Teheran announced fresh attacks in the region, with the Revolutionary Guards saying they hit a commercial ship in Bahrain allegedly linked to Israel.
The war erupted more than a month ago with US-Israeli strikes on Iran, triggering a retaliation that has spread the conflict throughout the Middle East and convulsed the global economy – particularly due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a vital conduit for oil and gas.
"Remember when I gave Iran ten days to MAKE A DEAL or OPEN UP THE HORMUZ STRAIT," Trump wrote on Truth Social, referring to an ultimatum issued on March 26.
"Time is running out – 48 hours before all hell will reign (sic) down on them."
Teheran said on Friday it had shot down an F-15 warplane and US media reported United States special forces had rescued one of its two crew members, with the other still missing.
Iran's military also said it downed a US A-10 ground attack aircraft in the Gulf, with US media saying the pilot of that plane was rescued.
The local Mehr news agency on Saturday quoted the deputy governor of Kohgiluyeh and Boyer-Ahmad province, Fattah Mohammadi, as saying the search for the missing pilot involved "presence of popular forces and tribesmen alongside military forces and is still ongoing."
He added that "last night, people fired at enemy helicopters with rifles and did not allow them to land."
Images posted on social media and verified by AFPTV showed Iranian police firing at a US helicopter in southwestern Iran as US forces searched for the airman.
Mohammad Ghalibaf, the speaker of Iran's parliament, mocked the Trump administration, saying the "war they started has now been downgraded from 'regime change' to 'Hey! Can anyone find our pilots?'"
"What incredible progress. Absolute geniuses."
Retired US Brigadier General Houston Cantwell, who has 400 hours of combat flight experience, said a pilot's training would likely kick in before he or she parachuted to the ground.
"My priority would be, first of all, concealment, because I don't want to be captured," he told AFP.
- Bushehr nuclear plant -
A strike near Iran's Bushehr nuclear plant on Saturday killed a guard and led Russia, which partly constructed the facility and helps operate it, to announce it was evacuating 198 workers.
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi warned that continued attacks on the plant on the southern coast could eventually lead to radioactive fallout that would "end life in GCC (Gulf Cooperation Council) capitals, not Teheran."
Bushehr is considerably closer to Kuwait, Bahrain and Qatar than it is to the Iranian capital.
Rafael Grossi, head of the International Atomic Energy Agency wrote on X that no increase in radiation levels had been reported at the site, but nonetheless voiced "deep concern" at what he said was the fourth such strike in recent weeks.
"NPP (nuclear power plant) sites or nearby areas must never be attacked," he said.
There were also more strikes on Teheran, where an AFP journalist saw a thick haze of grey smoke covering the skyline.
"This war wasn't for freedom... we just ended up trapped with something even more savage," 31-year-old Faezeh told AFP via messenger app from Teheran.
"They bomb randomly, there's no sign of any specific target these recent days."
Maryam, a 35-year-old from Khansar in Isfahan province said Iranians are divided between those hoping for an end to their government and those more fearful of economic disaster.
"I'm honestly really scared about our future," she told AFP. "Things are a disaster right now. Mass layoffs, widespread shutdowns... everything feels overwhelming."
Strikes by all sides have increasingly targeted economic and industrial sites, raising fears of wider disruption to global energy supplies.
US-Israeli strikes on Saturday hit a petrochemicals hub, a cement plant and a trade terminal on the Iran-Iraq border, where one person was reported killed.
Iran has retaliated with missile and drone attacks on Israel and US allies in the Gulf.
Shrapnel from intercepted drones injured four people in Bahrain on Saturday, and two buildings in Dubai were hit by debris, including one housing the US cloud computing firm Oracle, authorities said.
On their Sepah News website, Iran's Revolutionary Guards also said they had targeted a commercial vessel, the MSC Ishyka, "owned by the Israeli regime and flying the flag of a third country" at the Khalifa Bin Salman port in Bahrain.
- Beirut explosions -
On another front, the Israeli military said Friday it had struck more than 3,500 targets across Lebanon in the month since the latest round of fighting with Iran-backed Hizbollah began.
Lebanese state media reported that Israel destroyed a bridge in the Bekaa region, and local media said a second bridge was also hit, after Israel said it would strike them.
An AFP journalist heard two loud explosions in Beirut early Saturday and saw smoke billowing from one of them.
A hospital in the coastal Lebanese city of Tyre was damaged by Israeli strikes on nearby buildings that wounded 11 people, the health ministry said.
The Israeli military later issued an urgent evacuation warning to residents of the city ahead of more planned strikes.
Tens of thousands of people have left Tyre, but around 20,000 remain, including 15,000 displaced from surrounding villages. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Madani Mart runs on licensing model, no govt funding – Fuziah
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Madani Mart operates on a licensing model in collaboration with entrepreneurs and does not rely on government funding, says Deputy Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Minister Datuk Dr Fuziah Salleh.
She said that Yayasan Madani, as the brand owner, managed the ecosystem, including training, systems, pricing and retail analysis supported by artificial intelligence (AI).
She said the model allowed entrepreneurs flexibility to run their businesses, with supply chain support provided by Yayasan Madani.
"Yayasan Madani, which is aligned with the government, does not receive government funding and does not provide capital for Madani Mart. Each outlet is privately owned," she told reporters during a walkabout at a supermarket here today.
Her remarks came in response to queries on Madani Mart, launched yesterday, and claims that it is similar to Kedai Rakyat 1Malaysia (KR1M).
Yesterday, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim officiated the
first Madani Mart
outlet in Indera Mahkota, near Kuantan, Pahang.
The initiative, led by Yayasan Madani, introduces a community retail concept offering daily necessities at affordable prices under the slogan "Kedai Runcit Harga Rahmah."
Fuziah said Madani Mart represented a strategic approach to tackling cost-of-living challenges in a more comprehensive and targeted manner without government capital.
Instead, she said, the initiative was driven through collaboration between the ministry, private operators or licence holders, and Yayasan Madani.
She added that Madani Mart also provided opportunities for local communities and entrepreneurs to participate in the business.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israel strikes Tyre in south Lebanon after evacuation warnings
Article:
BEIRUT: Israel's military renewed its strikes on the southern Lebanese city of Tyre on Saturday after issuing evacuation warnings, following attacks on nearby buildings that damaged a hospital in the city.
Israel has carried out strikes across Lebanon and launched a ground invasion in the south after Hizbullah entered the war in the Middle East on the side of its backer Iran on March 2.
The Israeli army struck three buildings it had warned people to evacuate, according to Lebanon's state-run National News Agency (NNA).
An AFP correspondent said that a missile hit an 11-storey building northeast of Tyre, completely destroying it and reducing it to a pile of rubble that covered a nearby gas station.
A second raid on a five-storey building near the city leveled half of it, leaving the other half standing.
The third strike was on the Burj al-Shamali Palestinian refugee camp, southeast of the city.
Tens of thousands of people have left Tyre, but around 20,000 remain, including 15,000 displaced from surrounding villages, despite Israeli evacuation warnings covering most of the city and a broad swathe of the south.
Saturday's Israeli warning followed strikes that wounded at least 11 people, including three civil defence members, and damaged a major hospital, the health ministry in Beirut said.
The director of the Lebanese Italian Hospital told the NNA that it would "remain open to provide the necessary medical care" despite the damage.
Overnight strikes destroyed two buildings nearby, an AFP correspondent saw, shattering windows and also causing suspended ceilings to collapse in the hospital, management said.
A wave of attacks hit the Tyre area on Saturday, including one on its port that struck a small boat and damaged others moored nearby, the correspondent said.
Another Israeli airstrike targeted and completely destroyed a mosque in the town of Baraashit in the Bint Jbeil district, the NNA reported.
- 'Unacceptable' attacks -
Dawn strikes also targeted Beirut's southern suburbs, a largely evacuated Hizbullah stronghold that has been attacked repeatedly during more than a month of war.
[[nid:1410444]]
In a statement on Saturday, Israel's military said it had "completed an additional wave of strikes targetting command centres belonging to the Quds Force Lebanon corps in Beirut", referring to the Iranian Revolutionary Guards' foreign operations arm, and "two headquarters of the (Palestinian Islamic Jihad)."
After attacking a bridge in the West Bekaa region in eastern Lebanon on Friday "to prevent the transfer of reinforcements and military equipment", Israel hit it again on Saturday, destroying it completely, the NNA said.
West Bekaa is right above Lebanon's south, where Israeli troops have been advancing on the ground.
The NNA also reported that in Shebaa near the eastern side of the Israeli border, Israeli forces abducted a man at around 3:00 am on Saturday.
It was at least the third time Israeli forces have seized someone from south Lebanon after infiltrating their home since the war with Hizbullah began.
The Iran-backed group claimed responsibility Saturday for a series of attacks on northern Israeli towns and Israeli troops in Lebanese border towns, particularly Marun al-Ras, Hula and Ainata.
The war has displaced upwards of a million people in Lebanon and killed more than 1,300 people in the country, including 53 medics and three Indonesian UN peacekeepers in the south.
The United Nations force said on Friday that three peacekeepers were wounded in a blast inside a UN facility near Odaisse, and were rushed to hospital.
Jakarta slammed the incident as "unacceptable" after the UN office there confirmed the wounded were Indonesian.
Indonesia's government said "these events underscore the urgent need to strengthen protection for UN peacekeeping forces amid an increasingly dangerous conflict situation."
On Saturday, a UN security official told AFP that Israeli forces destroyed 17 surveillance cameras linked to UNIFIL's main headquarters in Naqura.
The UN peacekeeping force has been caught in the crossfire in southern Lebanon since the start of the war, with Hizbullah launching attacks on Israel and its troops, and Israeli forces pushing into border towns. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Two drown after waves capsize kayak at Pantai Merdeka
Article:
SUNGAI PETANI: Two people, a man and a woman in their 20s, were found drowned after they were believed to have fallen into the sea while kayaking at Pantai Merdeka, near here, this afternoon.
Kuala Muda police chief assistant commissioner Hanyan Ramlan said the bodies of both victims were discovered at about 7.30pm.
He said the victims were believed to be participants in a programme organised by a higher learning institution at the location.
"The incident is believed to have occurred at about 5pm while they were kayaking in a group, moving from one location to another.
"The victims are believed to have encountered strong waves, and their kayak is thought to have become entangled in a fisherman's net," he said when contacted by Bernama today.
He said several other kayaks were involved in the incident, and some had capsized, but the other occupants were successfully rescued.
He added that the bodies have been sent to the Sultan Abdul Halim Hospital (HSAH) here for post-mortem.
Investigations are ongoing to determine the exact cause of the incident.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Zahid: Govt on high alert amid energy crunch
Article:
BAGAN DATUK: The government is making every effort to address pressures arising from the global energy crisis following the conflict in West Asia, says Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi.
He said the commitment includes convening weekly meetings with industry leaders through the National Economic Action Council (NEAC) to provide briefings, offering input to council members, and discuss possible solutions.
"Their views are taken into account in the measures implemented by the government. Likewise, daily briefings are held live, and we hope all information can be conveyed to the people," said Zahid, who is also Rural and Regional Development Minister.
Speaking to reporters after attending the Bagan Datuk Member of Parliament's Hari Raya Aidilfitri celebration tonight, he said all parties must not remain in their comfort zones and should be prepared to face the ongoing crisis.
The Hari Raya Aidilfitri celebration, hosted by the Bagan Datuk MP, drew some 14,000 visitors.
Also present were Perak Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Saarani Mohamad, Higher Education Minister Datuk Seri Dr Zambry Abd Kadir, Deputy Home Minister Datuk Seri Dr Shamsul Anuar Nasarah, and Majlis Amanah Rakyat chairman Datuk Dr Asyraf Wajdi Dusuki.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sabah faces energy supply challenges – Fadillah
Article:
KUCHING: Sabah is set to face significant challenges in ensuring a stable energy supply due to its heavy reliance on diesel for power generation, says Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Fadillah Yusof.
He said that rising diesel prices and global supply constraints were expected to put pressure on the state's power generation capacity, potentially affecting domestic, industrial and business needs.
"This is what the government must focus on — how to help Sabah secure sufficient supply. Power generation is crucial not only for daily use by the people, but also to ensure the economy continues to grow and operate smoothly," he told reporters after attending the Kampung Samariang Batu Aidilfitri gathering here today.
Fadillah, who is also Energy Transition and Water Transformation Minister, said that in Peninsular Malaysia, energy generation relied largely on gas, which accounts for more than 30 per cent, as well as coal, whose supply is considered more stable, although ensuring a steady gas supply remains a priority.
He said that Sarawak was in a more stable position due to its early transition to green energy sources, particularly hydropower, which contributes about 60 per cent of the state's electricity generation, alongside solar and other renewable sources that reduce dependence on fossil fuels.
Earlier in his speech, Fadillah said the government would take proactive measures to safeguard the welfare of the people amid potential increases in the cost of living caused by uncertainties in global energy supply.
"These measures include close monitoring through regular meetings involving the Federal and state governments to coordinate actions in addressing any impact from the prolonged conflict in West Asia.
"Among the expected impacts is an increase in logistics costs due to rising oil prices, which could in turn drive up the prices of goods, especially imports, if the situation persists for three to six months," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Classic bicycles bring Hari Raya cheer to Muar residents
Article:
MUAR: A group of classic bicycle enthusiasts here drew attention this Hari Raya Aidilfitri by organising a unique "marhaban ride" from house to house, blending tradition with nostalgia.
Held in Kampung Bukit Naning, the lively event featured a colourful convoy of vintage bicycles, trishaws and other classic rides, as participants went from one home to another spreading festive cheer.
The programme, organised by the Johor Classic Bicycle Association, saw the participation of about 60 owners from several states, the TV3 Bulletin Utama reported.
Dressed in full traditional attire, the riders added to the festive atmosphere, making the celebration even more vibrant for villagers and onlookers alike.
The convoy visited 15 homes, where participants performed marhaban, a traditional form of praise and blessings, continuing a practice that dates back generations.
Participants said the annual event not only strengthens bonds within the community but also serves as a way to preserve memories of past traditions.
"This programme allows us to strengthen our ties, but it is also about remembering the practices of earlier generations," one participant said.
Another rider, reflecting on decades gone by, said such scenes were rarely seen in recent years.
"I grew up in the 1960s, and I haven't seen this kind of festive bicycle culture for a long time. Now at 61, it feels very lively again with so many types of bicycles reappearing," he said.
Organisers and participants expressed hope that the event would continue in the future, possibly on a larger scale, to keep the tradition alive for younger generations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tabung Haji launches 'Haji Lestari' initiative to promote zero-waste culture
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Lembaga Tabung Haji (TH) has introduced the "Haji Lestari" initiative for the 1447H/2026 haj season, aimed at cultivating a zero-waste culture among pilgrims through the management and recycling of used ihram garments.
The initiative, implemented for the first time, was launched today by Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Religious Affairs) Dr Zulkifli Hasan today.
He said the initiative was in line with the principles of Maqasid Syariah, strengthening efforts to preserve the environment while raising pilgrims' awareness of their shared responsibility in ensuring a cleaner and more sustainable haj.
He said that ihram garments worn by male pilgrims were often kept or discarded after the haj season.
"Through this initiative, unused textiles can be repurposed for the benefit of society and the environment, while also enhancing pilgrims' awareness of their shared responsibility in ensuring a cleaner and more sustainable haj," he said at the launch of the Federal Territories-level Premier Haj Course at the Tuanku Mizan Zainal Abidin Mosque here today.
Zulkifli said the "Haji Lestari" initiative would be implemented in two phases — in the Holy Land and domestically.
He said implementation in the Holy Land would focus on recycling efforts through the collection and processing of used ihram garments.
Collection points will be set up at pilgrims' accommodation in Makkah and at campsites in Mina to facilitate contributions of garments that pilgrims do not intend to bring home.
Domestically, the initiative will involve upcycling, whereby collected ihram garments will be processed into new products.
Pilgrims performing haj and umrah who wish to contribute used ihram garments may do so at selected TH branch offices nationwide beginning October 2026.
The programme is expected to be expanded in the future to include the collection of other items such as towels, bags and clothing, transforming used materials into resources that benefit both society and the environment.
Meanwhile, Zulkifli advised pilgrims to remain vigilant in light of current developments, including the geopolitical situation in the Middle East, and to comply with all instructions set by TH while in the Holy Land.
He said the Malaysian government and TH continued to monitor developments closely and were working with the Saudi Arabian authorities, which have assured the safety and welfare of haj pilgrims.
He added that all 31,600 Malaysian haj pilgrims this year were in the final phase of preparation through the Premier Haj Course, which is being conducted in stages at selected mosques nationwide from April 3 to 12.
"Alhamdulillah, through this course, TH is able to prepare prospective pilgrims mentally, physically and spiritually through practical manasik (haj ritual) modules such as tawaf, saie and the stoning of the jamrah, conducted in large groups to simulate actual conditions in the Holy Land," he said.
For this year's haj season, flights to the Holy Land are scheduled from April 18 to May 20, with return flights from June 4 to 30, involving 100 chartered flights to transport all Malaysian pilgrims.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Artemis may see space race among  firms
Article:
Artemis II mission is shaping up to be more than just the next step in returning humans to the moon — it is a key test of whether the agency's traditional contractor-built systems can remain viable in a rapidly shifting space industry.
The mission, launched on Wednesday evening from the Kennedy Space Center in Florida,  is sending astronauts around the moon for the first time in over 50 years.
It  is the first crewed flight of Boeing and Northrop Grumman's  Space Launch System (SLS) rocket and Lockheed Martin's Orion capsule.
Artemis II marks the moment when their reliability will be judged under the highest possible stakes: human flight.
The outcome of Artemis II could reshape the political narrative around Orion as well as SLS, the world's most powerful active rocket, which has faced persistent criticism over delays, ballooning costs and a relatively slow launch rate.
"The stakes are extremely high whenever there are astronauts on board," said Michael Leshock, equity research analyst at KeyBanc Capital Markets, adding that Artemis II is  "a critical validation moment" as Nasa evaluates proven commercial options.
A new wave of private rockets inspired by SpaceX's reusable Falcon 9 has challenged Nasa's thinking with the expendable SLS, a reincarnation of decades-old, Shuttle-era tech as the industry has focused on reusability in more recent years. Commercial players like Elon Musk's SpaceX and Jeff Bezos' Blue Origin are waiting in the wings.
Nasa chief Jared Isaacman announced last week that the agency intends to open the SLS mission — launching Artemis astronauts and cargo off Earth — to competitive bids from other companies for missions after Artemis V. That was one of many changes Isaacman  made to the Artemis programme in recent weeks.
He also cancelled plans to upgrade SLS with a more powerful upper stage meant for later Artemis missions, instead tapping United Launch Alliance — the joint rocket venture of Boeing and Lockheed — to use its less powerful Centaur upper stage.
"If they (Nasa) do include SpaceX or Blue Origin, it would give the US more flexibility in who they partner with in the future, as SpaceX and Blue Origin are already part of Artemis; it's just how much larger a part they can play," said Andrew Chanin, CEO of ProcureAM.
Analysts say the SLS programme is costly and is unlikely to be a viable long-term option for Nasa to return to the moon on a regular, cost-effective cadence.
That makes the high-profile Artemis II mission a critical validation point for the contractors behind the programme, as newer, lower-cost rockets try to prove their  reliability.
Each SLS launch is estimated to cost between US$2 billion and US$4 billion.  SpaceX's Starship and Blue Origin's New Glenn are far cheaper, though pricing could fluctuate by tens of millions of dollars depending on the mission complexity.
Nasa paid US$18 million for an initial New Glenn flight last year, according to contracting data. Space station company Voyager paid US$90 million for its planned Starship launch, according to a recent earnings report.
Nasa tried to impose SLS cost-reduction strategies in 2023, to little avail. Boeing and Northrop set up a joint venture at the time through which Nasa would hand its ownership of the rocket to the companies, encouraging them to sell the rocket commercially.
Nasa has begun incorporating commercial systems into its Artemis architecture, awarding SpaceX and Blue Origin central roles for each to develop lunar landers. Future missions could expand that reliance, raising questions about how long SLS will remain a cornerstone of the programme.
Still, not everyone is ready to write off the legacy systems.
"SLS still has a lot of congressional support, which makes it difficult to kill the programme," said Austin Moeller, director of equity research at Canaccord Genuity.
Starship has test-launched 11 times since 2023, but has not yet deployed payloads into orbit. SLS and Orion achieved a successful uncrewed test flight in 2022 around the moon and back.
The Trump administration's budget proposal last year sought to end SLS after Artemis III, but Senate Appropriations Committee chairman Ted Cruz   countered with a bill that cemented the rocket's role through Artemis V.
The writers are from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Thailand seizes 100,000 litres of diesel owned by Malaysian company in Sadao
Article:
SONGKHLA: Thai authorities on Saturday seized 100,000 litres of diesel belonging to a Malaysian logistics company in Sadao, Songkhla, and detained a Thai employee to assist in investigations.
Songkhla Governor Rattasart Chotchu said a public tip-off led to intelligence gathering and a joint operation involving the police, military and local authorities on Saturday morning.
During the operation, authorities discovered diesel concealed in underground storage tanks owned by the company.
"Investigations found that the company, which is owned by a Malaysian national, had concealed 100,000 litres of diesel without a valid licence," he said when contacted by Bernama.
He added that preliminary investigations indicated the company had sourced the diesel from Malaysia and was believed to have smuggled it into Thailand.
Rattasart said Region 9 police had seized the fuel and detained the company employee for investigation under the Fuel Control Act 1999 for possessing and concealing fuel without authorisation.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sabah to weigh WFH move at next cabinet meeting
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: The Sabah government will decide on the possible implementation of a work-from-home (WFH) arrangement at its next cabinet meeting.
Chief Minister Datuk Seri Hajiji Noor said the move would be considered following a directive issued at the federal level.
"At the federal level, the prime minister has directed that, starting April 15, many civil servants are likely to work from home to save not only energy but also costs.
"This is something that should be given attention not only by the government but also by the public.
"If necessary, we will implement it. We will see how the concept works," he told reporters after a Hari Raya Aidilfitri celebration with the media here.
Commenting on the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, Hajiji said the situation could have a broader impact on economies worldwide, including Malaysia and Sabah.
The Strait of Hormuz has faced disruptions following Iran's restriction of maritime traffic, triggered by last month's United States-Israeli strikes, leading to a surge in oil prices.
"In Sabah, we have 1,400MW of electricity capacity. Of that, 1,100MW comes from gas supplied by Petronas directly to the state.
"We will not be significantly affected in that aspect. Only about 300MW comes from diesel, which we are monitoring closely, as any increase in diesel prices will affect Sabah.
"There will be an impact if diesel prices rise. However, Sabah still receives diesel subsidies, which help ease the burden," he said, adding that the state government would continue to monitor developments.
Earlier, Chief Minister's Department Media and Communications director Datuk Lucy Irene Yong expressed appreciation for the commitment and dedication of media practitioners in disseminating information to the public across government, mainstream and social media platforms.
"We appreciate the role of the media in clearly explaining government policies while also conveying the people's voices to the authorities for action.
"However, while the media today is driven by speed, this should not come at the expense of accuracy," she said, adding that the Media and Communications Office would continue to ensure the timely and accurate dissemination of information related to the state government and the Chief Minister.
Among those present were Sabah Journalists Association president Mariah Doksil, Kota Kinabalu Journalists Association chairman Datuk Jimmy Goh, editors-in-chief, bureau chiefs and more than 200 media practitioners.
Also in attendance were Assistant Minister to the Chief Minister Datuk Isnin Aliasnih, Yayasan Sabah director Datuk Ghulam Khan Bahadar and heads of departments.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Stronger legal tools needed to fight fraud
Article:
Malaysia has no specific law for fraud.    But the Penal Code does have provisions for  cheating, criminal breach of trust, misappropriation and theft.
Other laws may apply, such   as the Companies Act 2016 for corporate fraud, the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act 2001 for laundering proceeds of fraud, and the Securities Commission Act 1993  for investment- and securities-related fraud.
The have been high-profile prosecutions under the Penal Code for cheating, CBT and misappropriation, highlighting  the usefulness of these provisions  in addressing serious fraud.
According to the Association of Certified Fraud Examiners (ACFE), a global anti-fraud organisation, fraud is  categorised into asset misappropriation, corruption and financial statement fraud.
Asset misappropriation is the most common form of fraud, making up about 86 per cent of reported cases, and involves the theft or misuse of an organisation's resources, such as cash or inventory.
Corruption is the misuse of ones position or influence for personal gain, including bribery and conflicts of interest.
Financial statement fraud is less common but more severe, as it involves the intentional misstatement or manipulation of financial data to mislead stakeholders, such as investors. It affects company valuation rather than individual assets.
Many organisations operate under the misconception that fraud is absent within their systems.
ACFE 2024 reports that organisations lose roughly five per cent of annual revenue to occupational fraud, which is often detected through tips rather than audits, underscoring the need for strong internal controls.
This five per cent  translates to  about US$4.7 trillion lost annually worldwide. The most common fraud schemes involve asset misappropriation.
Bukit Aman's Commercial Crime Investigation Department  data shows that telecommunications scams and fake investment schemes are the primary drivers of  financial losses.
Scams in Malaysia have reached alarming levels, with the police recording over 67,735 online cases between January and November last year, involving more than RM2.7 billion in losses, a significant increase from RM1.57 billion in 2024 and RM1.28 billion in 2023.
Most involve phone calls, romance scams, e-commerce fraud, bogus financing offers and non-existent loans or investments.
Ignorance is a root cause of many scams, as a lack of awareness makes individuals more vulnerable to fraudulent schemes. When people dismiss authorities' warnings, they become easier targets for fraudsters.
Fraud is often fuelled by high-pressure environments, lack of honest and effective leadership, and ego-driven, unethical cultures that normalise corruption.
Additionally, poor governance, low integrity, ineffective controls, lack of enforcement  and complex organisational settings create opportunities while reducing the chance of detection.
In relation to ineffective controls, certifications are meant to signal competence, standardisation and adherence to best practices.
Even the World Bank uses data analytics and technological tools to detect fraud in procurement and public administration.
ACFE emphasises strong controls, artificial intelligence and human oversight for modern fraud prevention.
AI-driven data monitoring can reduce fraud losses by up to 50 per cent, but human intervention is still needed  to manage risks and ensure ethical use.
Traditional controls such as anonymous reporting hotlines, surprise audits and employee training remain critical for  mitigation.
Malaysia's ranking in the annual Global Fraud Index (GFI) 2025 fell sharply from 34 to 86, indicating a significant increase in relative vulnerability to fraud.
This decline is a red flag signalling the need to strengthen fraud prevention and risk management measures. Singapore, Thailand and Indonesia also dropped in the GFI 2025 ranking.
As fraud becomes increasingly complex,   prevention requires honest leadership, strong internal controls, ethical corporate culture and the strategic use of technology.
Organisations should maintain checks and balances, segregate duties, conduct regular audits, leverage AI and data analytics to detect anomalies, protect whistleblowers, encourage anonymous reporting and provide training to raise awareness.
Consistent enforcement of governance, clear consequences and regular policy reviews are essential to address emerging risks including from the dark web.
The writer is professor of criminology and criminal justice at HELP University
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Erdogan says Mideast war has caused 'geostrategic impasse'
Article:
ANKARA: Turkiye's President Recep Tayyip Erdogan said the war in the Middle East had led to a "geostrategic impasse", during a telephone conversation with NATO chief Mark Rutte, his office said Saturday.
"President Erdogan said the process started by the intervention against Iran had led to a geostrategic impasse and that the international community had to redouble its efforts to bring an end to this war," said the statement.
Turkiye has attempted to mediate an end to the hostilities, notably through negotiations conducted with Pakistan and Egypt.
Erdogan said his country was also continuing efforts "to reach a peaceful outcome" to the conflict between Russia and Ukraine.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy arrived in Istanbul Saturday for talks with Erdogan.
A senior Ukrainian official told AFP that the talks would not only be about drone interceptors but also about security cooperation in general.
The Turkish presidency said on X that the talks would focus "efforts towards a ceasefire and a lasting solution." - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NST Leader: WFH shouldn't just be a relic of crisis
Article:
The work-from-home's (WFH) immediate aim is crisis-driven but the policy opens a bigger question: what if it actually works?
If WFH proves effective in maintaining productivity and service delivery, it could even be retained and become a structural reform of the civil service, modernising how the government operates in a digital era.
Around the world, remote and hybrid work have evolved into permanent features of efficient public administration.
Norway, a table-topper in many fields, offers an innovative example for others to follow. It has gone hybrid since the Covid-19 pandemic.
It even has a legal framework — the Work Environment Act 2022 — that requires employers to monitor working hours and ensure safety of workers, even at home.
What used to be sporadic WFH is now being driven by more structured hybrid models, ranging from 20 to 100 per cent home work.
Granted, it is not yet universal — with many companies requiring physical presence — but certainly a permanent new normal.
Ireland and the UK are two other examples of countries that have gone hybrid, with the latter topping Europe's list.
Current estimates say between 40 to 44 per cent workers are said to be involved in hybrid arrangements.
Malaysia, which has examples to follow, is now on a live policy experiment, and an opportunity.
On why it could work, civil service performance has long been tied to physical presence rather than measurable outcomes.
WFH forces a shift towards key performance indicators (KPIs), deliverables and accountability metrics.
A more flexible civil service could also attract younger, digitally native talent, retain skilled workers who might otherwise leave for private sector flexibility and enhance the government's competitiveness as an employer.
It could also enable the decentralisation of the workforce (less Klang Valley-centric), and support development beyond urban centres, as civil servants are less tied to physical offices.
That our workforce is Klang Valley-centric is a point well-made by environmental watchdog RimbaWatch.
Using data from the Statistics Department and other sources, RimbaWatch estimates the Klang Valley  hosts 4.1 million workers from the public and private sectors.
The watchdog says if 50 per cent of the workers were to go on WFH mode, Malaysia  would save 4.51 million litres of petrol a day or 90.2 million litres a month.
And here is what should please Putrajaya: at current fuel subsidy of RM1.87 per litre, this would amount to a savings of RM169 million a month.
Mandating WFH nationwide, RimbaWatch says, could save the country RM1 billion a month, depending on the number of workers able to work from home.
There are other benefits, too. Fuel consumption can be reduced, energy supply sustained and air pollution curbed, thus saving billions in healthcare expenses.
However, for WFH to be effective, some key weaknesses must be addressed, such as risk of abuse or reduced discipline; uneven digital infrastructure across agencies; management styles still rooted in supervision, not trust; and, public perception that services may slow down.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Former temple chairman detained over RM50,000 misappropriation
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Selangor Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) has detained a former temple chairman for alleged misappropriation of RM50,000 in funds.
According to a source, the man in his 70s was arrested at about 3pm on April 2 while at the Selangor MACC office to provide his statement.
"Preliminary investigations found that the suspect is believed to have committed the offence in November 2021 and had colluded with another person, who was detained last month, by submitting an invoice claiming RM50,000 for consultancy services related to repair works at the temple.
"However, the works are believed to have never been carried out and were merely a ploy to mislead the temple's management," the source said.
The source added that investigations are now focused on the temple, which has existed since the 1950s, with its primary source of income derived from donations and contributions by Hindu devotees.
It was also revealed that the temple's registration had been revoked by the Registrar of Societies (ROS) in October 2024, and that the structure was built on government land without proper authorisation.
"The temple's unregistered status resulted in a lack of oversight by authorities over its financial management and expenditure, thereby creating opportunities for the misappropriation of public donations.
"This misappropriation is believed to have affected the temple's operations and welfare, including disrupting religious activities and eroding devotees' trust in the institution's management," the source said.
Meanwhile, Selangor MACC director Mohd Azwan Ramli when contacted, confirmed the arrest and said the case is being investigated under Section 18 of the MACC Act 2009.
He added that both suspects are expected to be charged in the near future.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fugitive Naples mafia boss arrested for murder
Article:
ROME: An Italian mafia boss accused of murder has been arrested at a luxury villa on the Amalfi Coast, Italian police said on Saturday.
Roberto Mazzarella, head of the Mazzarella clan of the Camorra, the Naples-based organised crime group, was one of Italy's most dangerous fugitives, police said.
Mazzarella "did not resist arrest" during a raid in the town of Vietri sul Mare, police said in a statement. He was with his wife and two children at the time.
Video released by police of the raid showed heavily armed officers in the seaside villa. A patrol boat was deployed during the operation.
The 48-year-old had been on the run since January 28, 2025 when he was due to have been arrested on murder charges, the statement said.
He was on an interior ministry list of Italy's most dangerous fugitives.
Police said they found 20,000 euros ($23,000) in cash and three luxury watches during the raid.
The Mazzarella clan is famed for its involvement in counterfeiting in Naples, which has long been associated with banknote forgery.
Police last month detained 16 people allegedly linked to the Mazzarella clan on cyber fraud charges. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd
